questions.question: Explain the difference between AWT Button and Swing JButton.
questions.hint: Compare heavyweight vs lightweight and package differences
keyPoints: AWT Button is heavyweight, while JButton is lightweight
JButton extends JComponent, Button extends Component
JButton supports icons, mnemonics, and tool tips; Button does not
JButton uses Model-View-Controller architecture; Button does not
JButton is part of javax.swing package; Button is part of java.awt package

questions.question: What are the two key features of Swing that distinguish it from AWT?
questions.hint: Think about visual customization and component architecture
keyPoints: Pluggable Look and Feel (PL&F) - components can have different visual appearances
Lightweight components - all Swing components are lightweight except top-level containers
Components are written in pure Java - platform-independent
Uses MVC architecture for separation of data, view, and control

questions.question: Explain the Model-View-Controller (MVC) architecture as applied to Swing components.
questions.hint: Explain each component of MVC and how JButton implements it
keyPoints: Model - stores the data/state of the component (e.g., button state, text)
View - displays the component to the user (visual representation)
Controller - handles user interactions and updates the model
In Swing, Model and View are often combined (Model-Delegate)
JButton uses ButtonModel to store state (pressed, armed, armed, etc.)
Separation allows multiple views of the same data

questions.question: List and explain the different constructors available for creating JButton.
keyPoints: JButton() - creates button with no text or icon
JButton(String text) - creates button with specified text
JButton(Icon icon) - creates button with specified icon
JButton(String text, Icon icon) - creates button with both text and icon
Constructors can also accept Action object to set multiple properties at once

questions.question: Explain the event handling mechanism for JButton. What interface is used and what method is invoked?
questions.hint: Focus on ActionListener and actionPerformed method
keyPoints: JButton generates ActionEvent when clicked
ActionListener interface must be implemented
The actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) method is called when button is clicked
Use addActionListener() method to register the listener
The event object contains source of the event and action command

questions.question: What is the difference between heavyweight and lightweight components in Java Swing?
keyPoints: Heavyweight components use native peers from the underlying OS
Lightweight components are drawn entirely by Java without native peers
JButton is lightweight; top-level containers like JFrame are heavyweight
Lightweight components can have transparent backgrounds
Mixing heavyweight and lightweight can cause Z-order issues

questions.question: Describe the Swing component hierarchy. Explain how JButton is related to JComponent and Container classes.
questions.hint: Trace the inheritance chain from Object to JButton
keyPoints: Object (java.lang.Object) is the root
Component (java.awt.Component) - abstract base for all UI objects
Container (java.awt.Container) - can contain other components
JComponent (javax.swing.JComponent) - base for all Swing components
AbstractButton - provides common functionality for buttons
JButton extends AbstractButton which extends JComponent
JButton does not directly extend Container (cannot contain other components)
Hierarchy enables features like double buffering, borders, and tooltips

questions.question: Explain the event delegation model in Swing. How does it differ from the AWT event model?
questions.hint: Compare the older AWT model with Swing's delegation model
keyPoints: Event delegation model uses listeners instead of overrides
Event source generates events; listeners receive and process them
Swing uses EventDispatchThread for all UI updates
Swing supports additional event types: KeyStroke, Action, Change, etc.
Swing components are JavaBeans compliant with property change support
Swing uses javax.swing.event package for specialized events
All Swing event handling is thread-safe through EventDispatchThread
Uses addXxxListener() and removeXxxListener() methods

questions.question: Write a complete Swing program to create a JFrame with a JButton that displays the number of times it has been clicked. Include proper event handling and explain the code.
questions.hint: Focus on complete code with counter functionality
keyPoints: Create JFrame with setDefaultCloseOperation and setSize
Create JButton with appropriate constructor
Create JLabel to display click count
Implement ActionListener interface or use anonymous class
Use instance variable to track count
Update label text in actionPerformed method
Add button and label to content pane
Set layout manager (FlowLayout or BorderLayout)
Make frame visible with setVisible(true)

questions.question: Explain the purpose and usage of ImageIcon class in Swing. How is it used with JButton?
questions.hint: Cover both creation and state-specific usage
keyPoints: ImageIcon creates icons from images (GIF, JPEG, PNG)
It implements Icon interface and is Serializable
Can be created from file path, URL, or byte array
JButton constructor accepts ImageIcon directly
setIcon() method changes button icon at runtime
JButton supports different icons for states: pressed, disabled, rollover
setRolloverIcon() and setPressedIcon() for state-specific images
ImageIcon loads images asynchronously to prevent UI freezing

questions.question: Discuss the various methods available in JButton (or AbstractButton) for customizing button appearance and behavior.
questions.hint: Cover text, icon, keyboard, tooltip, and visual customization
keyPoints: setText(String) and getText() - for button text
setIcon(Icon) and getIcon() - for button icon
setMnemonic(int) - for keyboard shortcut (Alt+key)
setActionCommand(String) - to identify the action
setToolTipText(String) - for tooltip display
setEnabled(boolean) - to enable/disable button
setVerticalAlignment() and setHorizontalAlignment() - for alignment
setBorderPainted(), setContentAreaFilled(), setFocusPainted() - for visual customization
setRolloverEnabled(boolean) - for rollover effect

questions.question: Design a Swing application with two JButtons (Login and Cancel) placed in a JPanel using GridLayout(1,2). Write the complete code and explain the layout.
questions.hint: Use GridLayout with 1 row and 2 columns
keyPoints: Import javax.swing.* and java.awt.* packages
Create main class extending JFrame
Create JPanel with GridLayout(1,2) - 1 row, 2 columns
Create JButton loginBtn = new JButton("Login")
Create JButton cancelBtn = new JButton("Cancel")
Add buttons to panel using add() method
Add panel to frame's content pane
Set frame properties: size, title, default close operation
GridLayout distributes space equally among components

questions.question: Write a Swing program that creates a JButton with an image icon. Add an ActionListener that changes the button's text from 'Start' to 'Stop' and vice versa when clicked.
questions.hint: Use a boolean flag to toggle between states
keyPoints: Create JButton with text and icon: new JButton("Start", new ImageIcon("icon.png"))
Initialize boolean toggle variable (e.g., boolean isStarted = false)
Implement ActionListener with actionPerformed method
In actionPerformed, check toggle state using if-else
Use button.setText() to change text based on state
Use button.setIcon() to change icon based on state (optional)
Toggle the boolean: isStarted = !isStarted
Register listener: button.addActionListener(this) or anonymous class

questions.question: Create a simple calculator interface using JButton arranged in a 4x4 grid. Include number buttons (0-9), operation buttons (+, -, *, /), and equals (=) and clear (C) buttons.
questions.hint: Focus on layout structure and button arrangement
keyPoints: Create JFrame with title "Simple Calculator"
Create JTextField for display (non-editable or read-only)
Create JPanel with GridLayout(4,4) for buttons
Create buttons: 0-9 digits, +, -, *, /, =, C
Store buttons in array or create individually
Add buttons to panel in order (row by row)
Add panel to frame (typically SOUTH in BorderLayout)
Add text field to frame (typically NORTH in BorderLayout)
Set appropriate sizes and visibility

questions.question: What are the key packages in Swing? List at least five important Swing packages and their purposes.
keyPoints: javax.swing - core Swing components and classes
javax.swing.event - event classes and listeners specific to Swing
javax.swing.border - border implementations for components
javax.swing.plaf - pluggable look and feel interfaces and classes
javax.swing.table - JTable and related classes
javax.swing.tree - JTree and related classes
javax.swing.text - text component support
